question: What are the two primary purposes of creating a branch in GitHub, and how does the tutorial demonstrate both of these purposes?
answer: The two primary purposes of creating a branch are: (1) to add new features or make changes without altering the main source of code until ready; (2) to experiment with edits before committing them to the main branch. The tutorial demonstrates this by having the user create a 'readme-edits' branch to modify the README file separately from the 'main' branch, then merge it later via a pull request.
supporting_sentences: Branching is helpful when you want to add new features to a project without changing the main source of code. The work done on different branches will not show up on the main branch until you merge it, which we will cover later in this guide.
You can use branches to experiment and make edits before committing them to main.
Now you have two branches, main and readme-edits. Right now, they look exactly the same. Next you'll add changes to the new readme-edits branch.
These changes will be made only to the README file on your readme-edits branch, so now this branch contains content that's different from main.

question: How many distinct steps are involved in the process of creating and merging a pull request in this tutorial, and what are they in order?
answer: There are 6 distinct steps: (1) Create a branch; (2) Make and commit changes to the branch; (3) Open a pull request; (4) Review the changes in the diffs; (5) Merge the pull request; (6) Delete the branch after merging.
supporting_sentences: Click the Code tab of your hello-world repository. Above the file list, click the dropdown menu that says main. Type a branch name, readme-edits, into the text box. Click Create branch: readme-edits from main.

Under the readme-edits branch you created, click the README.md file. To edit the file, click the pencil icon. In the editor, write a bit about yourself. Click Commit changes. In the 'Commit changes' box, write a commit message that describes your changes. Click Commit changes.
Click the Pull requests tab of your hello-world repository. Click New pull request. In the Example Comparisons box, select the branch you made, readme-edits, to compare with main (the original).

Look over your changes in the diffs on the Compare page, make sure they're what you want to submit. Click Create pull request. Give your pull request a title and write a brief description of your changes.
At the bottom of the pull request, click Merge pull request to merge the changes into main. Click Confirm merge.
Click Delete branch. Now that your pull request is merged and your changes are on main, you can safely delete the readme-edits branch.

question: Under what condition would GitHub prevent merging a pull request, and what are the two ways the tutorial suggests to resolve this issue?
answer: GitHub would prevent merging a pull request if there are conflicts between the changes in the pull request and the existing code on the main branch. The tutorial suggests resolving this by: (1) making a commit that resolves the conflicts; (2) using comments in the pull request to discuss the conflicts with team members.
supporting_sentences: Sometimes, a pull request may introduce changes to code that conflict with the existing code on main. If there are any conflicts, GitHub will alert you about the conflicting code and prevent merging until the conflicts are resolved.
You can make a commit that resolves the conflicts or use comments in the pull request to discuss the conflicts with your team members.

question: What is the difference between the default branch name and the branch name created in the tutorial, and why was the latter created?
answer: The default branch name is 'main', while the branch created in the tutorial is named 'readme-edits'. The 'readme-edits' branch was created to make changes to the README file without affecting the 'main' branch, allowing the user to experiment and review changes before merging them into 'main'.
supporting_sentences: By default, your repository has one branch named main that is considered to be the definitive branch.
Type a branch name, readme-edits, into the text box. Click Create branch: readme-edits from main.
Now you have two branches, main and readme-edits. Right now, they look exactly the same. Next you'll add changes to the new readme-edits branch.
These changes will be made only to the README file on your readme-edits branch, so now this branch contains content that's different from main.

question: What are the three optional files or features GitHub offers when creating a new repository, and which of these does the tutorial explicitly instruct the user to include?
answer: The three optional files or features are: (1) a README file; (2) a license file; (3) other common options (not specified). The tutorial explicitly instructs the user to include a README file.
supporting_sentences: GitHub lets you add a README file at the same time you create your new repository. GitHub also offers other common options such as a license file, but you do not have to select any of them now.
Select Add a README file. Click Create repository.
